---
title: "Day 5: Onshape to Blender"
subtitle: "Morning brief, Friday July 17"
author: "Dan Negrut"
date: 2026-07-17
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: [default, ../theme.scss]
    slide-number: true
    width: 1250
    margin: 0.08
    transition: fade
    footer: "ProCSI C Training  |  Day 5"
    embed-resources: true
---

## Where today's work fits in the big picture

- This is how engineers actually work: design a part in **CAD**, then hand it to other tools for rendering, analysis, or simulation.
- **Onshape** is professional CAD that runs in the browser; you already have your free accounts.
    - If you already know SolidWorks or Fusion 360, feel free; Onshape is the free, browser-based option.
- Today closes week 1: CAD in the morning, and your day-4 Blender skills bring the design to life in the afternoon.

## Today's target

- **By 3:30, your team can:** show a mechanism (a **slider-crank**, a **four-bar linkage**, a **robot arm**, etc.) modeled in Onshape, exported as **OBJ**, imported into Blender, and rendered as a nice still image.
- **Bonus:** every part has its own material or color in the render.

## How to start

- Sign in at **onshape.com**; create a new document.
- Sketch a 2D profile, **extrude** it into a part; repeat for each link of your mechanism.
- Put the parts together in an **Assembly** using **mates** (revolute mate for a pin joint, slider mate for the piston).
- **No "starter slides" today.** Use Onshape's built-in tutorials & Claude.
- Ask Claude: "Explain sketch, extrude, and mate in Onshape like I'm brand new" or "What is a four-bar linkage? Draw me the idea in words."

## Export your model (Blender & Chrono) {.smaller}

- In Onshape: right-click the tab (Part Studio or Assembly), **Export**, choose **OBJ**.
    - Set **units to meters** (Chrono works in meters; a part exported in mm comes in 1000x too big).
    - Keep the **`.obj` and its `.mtl` together**; color and material live in the `.mtl`.
- In Blender: **File, Import, Wavefront (.obj)**; if it comes in huge or tiny, press **S** to scale.
- Right-click the object, **Shade Smooth**, so curved parts stop looking like disco balls.
- Then it is yesterday's skills: material, light, camera, **F12**, save the PNG.
- **Why OBJ?** Chrono reads it directly next week (its meshes are `.obj`), so one export serves both Blender and Chrono.

## Also: two lunar rocks (in Blender) {.smaller}

- Onshape is great for machined parts, but rocks are organic; **Blender** is the right tool here.
- In Blender, make **two lunar rocks**, about **0.4 m** and **0.2 m** across.
    - Easy path: add an icosphere, then a **Displace** modifier with a noise texture (or a rock-generator add-on). Ask Claude.
    - See how Moon rocks look: [Apollo lunar samples](https://ares.jsc.nasa.gov/astromaterials3d/apollo-lunar.htm).
- **Export each as OBJ (in meters)** and email them to Dan; we'll drop them into the lab's simulations and show them next week.

## Your end-of-day talk

- ~10 minutes: show the Onshape model (drag the mechanism around live if you can) and your Blender render.
- **Start or Close with your team's quote of the day.**
- Stretch goal: in Onshape, animate the mate that drives your mechanism and screen-record it moving.
- Heads up (today only): talks run **3:30 to 4:00**, then the **photo shoot at 4:00** in the Mechanical Engineering atrium.
